reproducible_artifact: Code-review checklist item: 'For any PR by a senior IC who has been granted carve-outs from process artifacts (style-guide citation, ADR cross-reference, runbook ack), the reviewer spot-checks the PR for adherence to the rules the carve-out covers; if adherence is high, the carve-out stands; if adherence is low, the carve-out is revoked and the artifact requirement returns.'
embodiment_sketch: A team-level PR template adds a section: '## Carve-out audit (senior IC only)'. Below it: a checkbox list per granted carve-out — `[ ] Lint rules (eslint:recommended) — last 5 PRs reviewed for adherence on YYYY-MM-DD`. A monthly retro reviews the carve-out audit log; if the audit shows the engineer's output is no longer rule-fluent, the carve-out is revoked. The runbook artifact is a Confluence page named 'Carve-out audit ledger' with one row per (engineer, rule-set, last-audit-date, adherence-rate, status). Pseudocode for the audit predicate: `function auditCarveOut(engineerPRs, ruleSet, threshold=0.95) { return ruleSet.every(rule => adherenceRate(engineerPRs, rule) >= threshold); }`
verdict_reason: The verse's claim ('the rule has dissolved into your judgment correctly if your output still expresses it; mere forgetting if not') is directly encodable as a periodic audit of de-cited fluency vs. artifact quality. The artifact is not a metaphor — it is a concrete carve-out ledger plus a measurable adherence predicate. A senior engineer could literally add this to their team's practice on Monday: PR template field, monthly audit cadence, ledger Confluence page, revocation rule. The 'two years into the team' figure in the concrete_scenario is metaphorical, but the implication clause translates directly to a measurable audit. Pass.
